Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 33 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 6 actions returned the board to a state already seen 6 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 6 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 5 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
The board state hasn't changed visually - it still shows the same arrangement. But the action did change the board (gameplay_changed=true). Let me check what actually changed by looking at the segmentation. It seems like clicking on the empty slot placed M there, but visually it looks the same as before because M (magenta/pink) might look similar to N (light green) in the ASCII rendering.
Wait, looking at the image again, the lower frame still shows: blue, green, yellow, gray. The middle slot still shows green (N), not magenta (M). So the action didn't place M there.
Actually, looking more carefully at the image provided, I see the lower frame has:
- Blue square
- Green square (N)
- Yellow square
- Gray empty slot
This is the SAME as before. But the action reported gameplay_changed=true. Let me check what actually changed.
Actually wait - I removed N from the lower frame, then clicked the empty slot. But M was supposed to be selected. Let me check if M is still selected or if something else happened.
Let me look at the current state more carefully using segmentation.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what's actually in the lower frame now
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Current placed colors:")
for n in nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
    min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
    if 20 <= min_r <= 45 and n['pixels'] >= 10:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, top=({min_r},{min_c})")

# Check palette
print("\nPalette area (row 60+):")
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for i in range(58, 64):
    if i < len(ascii_lines):
        print(f"Row {i}: {ascii_lines[i]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
Looking at the current state:
- Upper: O, p, R (with N L-shape)
- Lower: b, N, Y
- Palette: M (MMMM visible at row 58-60)
So the state is the same as before. I removed N and then placed M, but it seems like M was placed in the slot where N was, making the state: b, M, Y. But the segmentation shows N at (35, 27), not M. 
Wait, let me re-read. The segmentation shows:
- color=N, pixels=16, top=(35,27) - this is in the lower frame
So N is still in the lower frame. But I clicked on N to remove it, then clicked on the empty slot to place M. The fact that N is still there means either:
1. The first click didn't remove N (but it reported gameplay_changed)
2. The second click placed N back (but M was selected)
Actually, looking at the palette, M (MMMM) is shown. But when I clicked on the empty slot, maybe the game placed whatever was in the palette at that position. But N is showing in the lower frame...
Wait, I'm confused. Let me trace through what happened:
1. Before: Lower frame had b, N, Y
2. I clicked on N at (36, 28) - this removed N, making the slot empty
3. I clicked on the empty slot at (36, 28) - this should have placed M there
But the current state shows N at (35, 27). This is strange. Unless... clicking on the empty slot with M selected actually placed N there? That doesn't make sense.
Or maybe the two clicks (remove N, place M) happened to cancel each other out in terms of visual appearance but changed something internally?
Actually, looking more carefully at the palette row 58:
ccccccccccggcccccggcccccggcccccggccccMMMMccccggcccccggcccccccccc
The "MMMM" is the selected color indicator. But there are also "gg" patterns which might be unselected palette slots. Let me look at the full palette.
Hmm, I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just try a completely different approach. Instead of trying to figure out the exact slot mechanics, let me try pressing SPACE with the current arrangement to see if it completes the level. If not, I'll try ACTION7.
